title: Food & Restaurants
shortSummary: Order food and drinks, ask for the bill, and navigate a Turkish restaurant or cafe.
culturalNote: Food is at the heart of Turkish hospitality, and a guest is rarely allowed to leave hungry. A typical meal begins with meze, small shared plates, and tea (çay) follows almost everything; it is served black in small tulip-shaped glasses, and Turks drink it all day long. When ordering, you say what you want followed by lütfen (please), and a polite waiter is addressed with a simple bakar mısınız? (could you look here?). Turkish coffee (Türk kahvesi) is thick, served with a glass of water, and ordered by sweetness: sade (no sugar), az şekerli (little sugar), or şekerli (sweet). To say you have eaten well, the lovely phrase elinize sağlık (health to your hands) thanks whoever cooked. Tipping around five to ten percent is normal in restaurants if a service charge is not already included.

keyPhrases.english: Health to your hands (thanks to the cook)

grammar.rule: The accusative -i for a specific object
grammar.explanation: When you order a specific, definite thing, Turkish marks it with the accusative ending -i/-ı/-u/-ü (chosen by vowel harmony). Bunu istiyorum (I want this) uses bu plus -nu. A general, non-specific order leaves the noun bare: çay istiyorum (I want tea, tea in general).

examples.en: I want the bill (this specific one).

grammar.commonMistake: Adding the accusative to a general order. Say su istiyorum, not suyu istiyorum, unless you mean a specific water.

grammar.rule: Asking politely with the question particle mı
grammar.explanation: Turkish forms yes/no questions with the separate particle mı/mi/mu/mü, placed after the relevant word and following vowel harmony. To call a waiter you say bakar mısınız? (would you look?). The particle never attaches as a written suffix; it stays a separate word.
grammar.commonMistake: Forgetting to harmonise the particle. After 'acı' it is acı mı, but after 'tuzlu' it is tuzlu mu.

dialogue.setup: A customer sits down at a lokanta (casual restaurant) and orders dinner from the waiter.

lines.speaker: Garson
lines.native: Buyurun. Bugün mercimek çorbası çok güzel.
lines.english: Here you are. The lentil soup is very nice today.

lines.speaker: Müşteri
lines.native: Bir çorba ve bir köfte istiyorum. Et yemediğimi söyleyemem!
lines.english: I'd like a soup and a kofte. I can't say I don't eat meat!

lines.speaker: Garson
lines.english: Of course. What would you like to drink?

lines.speaker: Garson
lines.english: Coming right up. Enjoy your meal!

quiz.question: How do you ask for 'The bill, please' in Turkish?
quiz.explanation: Hesap is the bill; lütfen is please.

quiz.question: Which phrase calls the waiter politely?
quiz.explanation: Bakar mısınız? literally means 'would you look?' and is the standard way to get attention.

quiz.question: Bir çay, _____. (One tea, please)
quiz.explanation: Lütfen means 'please' and comes at the end of the request.

quiz.question: What do you say to thank whoever cooked?
quiz.explanation: Elinize sağlık means 'health to your hands', a warm compliment to the cook.

quiz.question: Arrange: istiyorum / bunu
quiz.explanation: Bunu istiyorum means 'I want this', with the object before the verb.
